rule.title: Issue Severity Classification
rule.description:
Analyze and prioritize issues by severity:
BLOCKER (Highest Priority) - Bug that represents a critical security vulnerability - Code that will cause application crash or data loss - Must be fixed immediately before deployment - Examples: SQL injection, null pointer dereference in critical path
CRITICAL - Bug with high probability of impacting production - Security vulnerability that could be exploited - Should be fixed before next release - Examples: Resource leaks, unhandled exceptions, weak cryptography
MAJOR - Bug that could impact production under specific conditions - Code smell that significantly impacts maintainability - Should be fixed in current sprint - Examples: Unused variables, complex methods, duplicated code
MINOR - Code smell with minor impact on maintainability - Can be fixed in future sprints - Examples: Missing javadoc, naming conventions
INFO - Informational findings - Best practice suggestions - Lowest priority

rule.title: Issue Type Categories
rule.description:
Categorize issues by type:
BUG - Code that is demonstrably wrong or highly likely to yield unexpected behavior - Focus: Reliability - Examples: Null pointer exceptions, resource leaks, logic errors
VULNERABILITY - Security-related issues that could be exploited - Focus: Security - Examples: SQL injection, XSS, weak encryption, hardcoded credentials
CODE_SMELL - Maintainability issues that make code harder to understand or modify - Focus: Maintainability - Examples: Complex methods, duplicated code, long parameter lists
SECURITY_HOTSPOT - Security-sensitive code that requires manual review - Focus: Security review - Examples: Authentication, authorization, data validation, cryptography

rule.title: Remediation Guidelines
rule.description:
Provide actionable remediation for common issues:
NULL POINTER EXCEPTIONS - Add null checks before dereferencing - Use Optional for potentially null values - Apply @NonNull/@Nullable annotations
RESOURCE LEAKS - Use try-with-resources for AutoCloseable objects - Ensure finally blocks close resources - Use Java 21 structured concurrency patterns
SQL INJECTION - Use PreparedStatement with parameterized queries - Never concatenate user input into SQL - Use ORM frameworks properly
COMPLEX METHODS - Extract methods to reduce complexity - Apply Single Responsibility Principle - Refactor nested conditionals
DUPLICATED CODE - Extract common code into reusable methods - Create utility classes for shared functionality - Use inheritance or composition appropriately
SECURITY VULNERABILITIES - Update dependencies to patched versions - Use secure coding practices - Apply principle of least privilege - Validate and sanitize all inputs

rule.title: Technical Debt Analysis
rule.description:
Analyze and report technical debt:
CALCULATION - Technical Debt = Effort to fix all code smells - Measured in days (e.g., "5d 3h") - Debt Ratio = Technical Debt / Development Cost
ACCEPTABLE LEVELS - Debt Ratio < 5%: Excellent - Debt Ratio 5-10%: Good - Debt Ratio 10-20%: Acceptable - Debt Ratio > 20%: Needs attention
PRIORITIZATION 1. Fix issues in frequently changed files first 2. Address high-severity code smells 3. Reduce complexity in critical paths 4. Eliminate duplications in core modules
REPORTING - Include debt ratio in quality reports - Track debt trend over time - Identify modules with highest debt - Suggest debt reduction strategies
